7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

HTTP QUERY MethodA safe, idempotent, cacheable request method that finally lets you send a body — here’s everything you need to know, with real examples.

Every backend engineer has hit this wall. You build a search endpoint. It starts simple: a keyword, maybe a limit. Then product wants filters. Then nested filters. Then sorting, pagination, date ranges, and a dozen optional facets. Suddenly your neat little URL looks like a ransom note, and you’re staring down two bad choices.
For roughly thirty years, HTTP gave us exactly two tools for this job, and both were wrong in their own way. As of June 2026, that changed: the IETF published RFC 10008, standardizing a brand-new HTTP method called QUERY. This is the first genuinely new, general-purpose HTTP method to reach Standards Track in a long time, and if you build or consume APIs, it’s worth understanding now — before your framework quietly turns it on.
Let’s break down what it is, the problem it solves, and how to actually use it.

The Two Bad Options We’ve Lived With
Option 1: GET with a query string
Semantically, GET is correct for search. It’s safe (it doesn’t change server state), idempotent (repeating it is harmless), and cacheable. The classic pattern looks like this:
GET /feed?q=foo&limit=10&sort=-published HTTP/1.1
Host: example.orgThe trouble starts when the query grows. URLs have practical length limits — proxies, load balancers, and servers often cap them at a few kilobytes, and the HTTP spec only recommends support for at least 8000 octets. Encoding arrays and deeply nested conditions into a query string is painful and unreadable. And there’s a privacy angle too: URLs get logged, cached, and saved in browser history and bookmarks — so your query parameters end up in a lot of places you didn’t intend.
Option 2: POST with a body
So most teams reach for POST instead, tucking the search parameters into a nice clean JSON body:
POST /feed HTTP/1.1
Host: example.org
Content-Type: application/json{ "q": "foo", "limit": 10, "sort": "-published" }This works — but you’ve quietly lied about your intent. POST means “this might change state.” It is not guaranteed safe or idempotent, so caches won’t cache it and clients won’t automatically retry it after a dropped connection. Nothing in the request tells an intermediary “hey, this is just a read.” You’ve traded semantics for convenience.
The tempting-but-cursed Option 3: GET with a body
“Fine,” you think, “I’ll just send a body on my GET.” Technically no RFC outright forbids it — but the specs strongly advise against it, and implementations disagree wildly. Some servers reject it, some silently drop the body, some read it. A user behind a corporate proxy might get a completely different result than one who isn’t. It’s a portability minefield. This is also why there’s no new RFC simply blessing GET-with-a-body: doing so would break countless existing systems.

Enter QUERY
The QUERY method threads the needle. In the words of the spec, it “spans the gap between the use of GET and POST.” That same search now looks like this:
QUERY /feed HTTP/1.1
Host: example.org
Content-Type: application/json{ "q": "foo", "limit": 10, "sort": "-published", "filters": ["status:active", "type:post"] }Like POST, the input travels in the request body, so there are no URL length limits and no ugly encoding. Unlike POST, QUERY is explicitly safe and idempotent — which means caching and automatic retries are back on the table. You get the clean body of POST with the honest semantics of GET.
Here’s the whole story in one table, adapted from the RFC:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That middle column is the whole point: QUERY takes the best column from each of its neighbors.

How It Actually Works
A QUERY request asks the target resource to process the enclosed content and respond with the result. The body plus its media type define the query — so the server treats a Content-Type as mandatory. A few rules worth internalizing:

• Content-Type is required. A QUERY with no media type, or a type inconsistent with the body, must be rejected with a 4xx. No content-sniffing allowed.

• It’s safe. The client neither requests nor expects any change to the target resource’s state.

• It’s idempotent. Dropped connection? The client can just retry — no worrying about half-applied writes.
Content negotiation and status codes
The RFC is refreshingly precise about failure modes, which makes for cleaner API contracts:

• 400 Bad Request — missing media type, or a body that doesn't match the declared type.

• 415 Unsupported Media Type — the server doesn't understand the query format you sent.

• 422 Unprocessable Content — the format is fine and understood, but the query itself doesn't make sense (think: syntactically valid SQL referencing a table that doesn't exist).

• 406 Not Acceptable — you asked (via Accept) for a response format the server can't produce.
Discovering support: the Accept-Query header
Because QUERY bodies can be anything — JSON, form-encoded data, SQL, JSONPath, XSLT — servers advertise which query formats they accept using a new response header:
Accept-Query: "application/json", "application/sql"That lets clients discover, up front, how they’re allowed to talk to an endpoint.
Turning a query into a bookmarkable URL
This is one of the most elegant parts of the spec, and it’s worth slowing down on because it trips people up. A successful QUERY response can carry one of two location headers, and they point at two different things:
Content-Location — a URL for the result. The server is telling you: "I've saved the answer I just computed at this address; GET it to retrieve that same result." It's a link to the output, frozen as it was.
QUERY /orders HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.org
Content-Type: application/json{ "status": "active", "limit": 10 }→ 200 OK
Content-Location: /results/xyz789Now GET https://api.example.org/results/xyz789 returns that specific snapshot of results.
Location — a URL for the query itself. This one is unique to QUERY. It points at a resource that re-runs your query when you GET it, returning fresh data each time — without you resending the body.
QUERY /orders HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.org
Content-Type: application/json{ "status": "active", "limit": 10 }→ 200 OK
Location: /saved-queries/42Later, GET https://api.example.org/saved-queries/42 re-executes "give me 10 active orders" against the current data. New orders show up; the question was remembered, not the answer.
A simple way to hold the two apart: Content-Location is the answer (a saved copy of the result), while Location is the saved question (re-run it anytime). And notice why POST can't cleanly offer that Location trick — replaying a POST isn't safe, so "here's a URL to repeat this request" would be dangerous. Because QUERY is safe and idempotent, it finally is.
This honors the old REST principle that important things deserve URIs — without forcing every search permutation into the address bar. In practice it means you can share a query link with a colleague, bookmark a search, or poll the same URL on a schedule for fresh results.

Can You Use It Today? (Yes, Carefully)
This is the part to be honest about: QUERY is standardized, but ecosystem support is still early. The method is a Proposed Standard, not something every browser, proxy, and CDN handles yet. Realistically, universal support will take years.
That said, adoption has already started:

• .NET 10 (LTS, released November 2025) ships with built-in QUERY support on both the client and server side — one of the first major platforms to do so.

• API tooling is following: clients like Kreya added out-of-the-box QUERY support in recent releases.

• Because an HTTP method is, technically, just a string, many frameworks let you wire up a custom QUERY handler today even without first-class support.
A pragmatic adoption strategy
Don’t rip out every search endpoint this weekend. A saner path:

• Start internally. Service-to-service calls, where you control both ends and the network path, are the perfect low-risk proving ground.

• Keep a fallback. Offer POST alongside QUERY until you’re confident every intermediary between client and server behaves.

• Mind your infrastructure. Old proxies, WAFs, and CDNs may not recognize the method and could reject or mangle it. Test the whole path, not just your app server.

• Set the cache key correctly. QUERY is cacheable — but the cache key must incorporate the request body, or you’ll serve wrong results. Verify your caching layer understands this.

Why This Matters
QUERY isn’t flashy. It won’t trend on tech Twitter the way a new framework does. But it fixes a genuine, decades-old design flaw in how we build search and reporting endpoints — the kind of quiet, foundational improvement that you stop noticing precisely because it just works.
The next time you catch yourself base64-encoding a filter object into a query string, or slapping POST on an endpoint that never writes a thing, remember: there’s finally a right answer. It’s called QUERY.

TL;DR

• RFC 10008 (June 2026) defines QUERY, a new HTTP method.

• It’s safe, idempotent, and cacheable like GET, but carries a request body like POST.

• It solves complex/large search queries that don’t fit in a URL and shouldn’t abuse POST.

• Content-Type is mandatory; the new Accept-Query header advertises supported formats.

• A response can return a Location (re-run the query) or Content-Location (fetch the saved result).

• Support is early but real — .NET 10 leads. Adopt gradually, keep a POST fallback, and test your full infrastructure path.

If this helped, give it a clap and follow for more deep dives on backend engineering, DevOps, and the plumbing that quietly runs the web.
Further reading: RFC 10008 (rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc10008) · IETF HTTP Working Group.
